config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society’s interests should automatically come before individual rights, because that can easily become an excuse for abuse by governments or majorities. In my view, rights like freedom, due process, and dignity are the foundation that keeps society fair in the first place. From my perspective, the better approach is to balance both, but never treat individual rights as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society matter, but they should not take automatic priority over individual rights. From an American perspective, I am wary of any argument that gives the collective a blank check, because history shows that governments and majorities can abuse that power very quickly when rights are treated as optional. At the same time, I do not think individual rights are absolute in every case, since public safety, public health, and basic rules do require some limits. The real standard should be narrow, justified restrictions, not a default assumption that society gets to override the person.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective as someone from Indonesia, I have seen how easily “the public good” can be used to silence criticism, justify corruption, or pressure minorities, so I do not trust collective interests as a blank check. Society does matter, especially in crises like health or safety, but its interests should only limit rights in narrow, clearly justified ways with real accountability. If individual rights are treated as secondary by default, then society stops being fair and becomes just the rule of the strongest majority.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take automatic priority over the rights of the individual. From my American perspective, that kind of rule feels dangerous because majorities and governments have a long history of using "the common good" to excuse overreach, censorship, and discrimination. I do think society can justify limited restrictions in cases like public safety or public health, but only when those limits are narrow, necessary, and accountable. If rights are treated as secondary by default, then they are not really rights at all.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take automatic priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how appeals to “the common good” can be used to pressure minorities, silence criticism, or excuse bad governance, so I do not trust collective interests as a default trump card. Society does matter for public safety, health, and order, but those limits must be narrow, justified, and accountable, not based on the assumption that the majority is always right. If individual rights can be overridden whenever it is convenient, then they are not real rights anymore.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically outrank the rights of the individual, and I do not trust that principle as a default rule in the United States. Our history is full of examples where "the common good" was used to justify censorship, discrimination, surveillance, and other abuses, so I am very skeptical of giving society a blank check. That said, I do not believe rights exist in a vacuum; public safety, public health, and basic civic order can justify limited restrictions when they are narrow, necessary, and accountable. If society is allowed to override rights whenever it wants, then those rights are just privileges.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
